segments.s578-g57: Poetic inversion: the predicate 'گنهکار باشد' (may/let be sinful) precedes the noun phrase 'تن زیردست' (the body/one under command). 'باشد' is the jussive/subjunctive/courtly present (lit. 'may be' / 'let it be'), so the whole clause expresses concession or toleration. 'تن زیردست' literally 'the body under (one's) hand' = the subordinate person/subject of rule.
segments.s578-g58: مگر here means 'unless' (introducing the exception). 'مردم' is the genitival/subject group 'the people'; 'پاک و یزدان‌پرست' are coordinate adjectives meaning 'pure and God‑worshipping/pious'. The clause gives the condition that would prevent accepting a sinful subordinate.
segments.s578-g59: Literal order: 'دل و مغز' (heart and brain) is the subject, 'مردم' marks them as 'of the people' (genitive). 'دو شاه' is the predicative nominal 'two kings'. 'تنند' functions as the copular/predicative ending in this archaic/poetic line ('they are [of] the body' / 'of the body they are kings'), so the phrase means 'are two kings of the body.'
segments.s578-g60: Parallel construction: 'دگر' = 'other' modifies 'آلت تن' = 'members/organs of the body'. 'سپاه' is a metaphorical predicate 'an army' and again 'تنند' is the poetic copular/identifier tying the phrase to 'of the body' (i.e. 'the other members are the body's army').
segments.s578-g61: چو = 'when' introduces a temporal clause. 'مغز و دل مردم' = 'the brain and heart of the people' (subject). 'آلوده' is an adjectival predicate 'defiled/tainted' and 'گشت' (from گشتن) is the perfective/past 'became' — together 'became defiled.'
segments.s578-g62: This is a result/secondary clause with an instrumental/causal sense: 'به' here = 'by/through', 'نومیدی' = 'despair', 'از رای' = 'of/from counsel (i.e. of any advice/plan)'. 'پالوده گشت' = 'was/ became purified' (پالوده = purified, گشت = became). The subject is understood from the previous clause (the people's heart and brain).
segments.s578-g63: 'بدان' is the compound of به + آن = 'to/at that'. It governs 'تن' ('that body/person'). 'سراسیمه' is an adjectival predicate 'frantic/upset' and 'گردد' (poetic form of گشتن) = 'becomes'. 'روان' = 'soul/spirit' is the subject/patient that becomes frantic. The clause means 'at that body the soul becomes frantic.'
segments.s578-g64: 'سپه' = 'the army' (subject). 'چون' = 'like/as' introduces the simile. 'زید شاه' = 'Zayd, the king' (apposition/name). 'بی‌پهلوان' is an adjectival predicate 'without a champion' (i.e. lacking a hero/champion). The clause reads 'the army, like Zayd the king, is without a champion.'
segments.s578-g25: من is the first‑person subject 'I'. این تاج شاهی is the direct object: تاج 'crown' modified by شاهی (adjectival/attributive 'royal' / 'of kingship'). سپارم is the first‑person present/optative form of سپردن ('I entrust / I will entrust'); the prepositional phrase به تو 'to you' marks the indirect object. Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect but reads as 'I entrust this royal crown to you.'
segments.s578-g26: همان here means 'the same' / 'that very (aforementioned)'. گنج و لشکر are coordinated direct objects 'treasure and army'. گذارم is the first‑person present of گذاردن/گذاشتن used in parallel with سپارم ('I put/place/entrust'); به تو again marks the beneficiary. The clause parallels the previous one (same subject, new objects).
segments.s578-g27: بپذرفت is simple past 3rd person singular 'he accepted / admitted'. The following ز (short for از) = 'from' and the next item (written here as و but functioning as او) supply the source 'from him' (poetic contraction زِ او → زُو). این سخن اردشیر is the object 'this speech/statement of Ardashir'. The clause thus means 'he accepted this speech of Ardashir from him.'
segments.s578-g28: به پیش is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before'. بزرگان is the plural 'great ones / nobles'. The repeated پیش before دبیر is a stylistic parallel, and دبیر here means the royal scribe/secretary. Altogether: 'in the presence of the nobles and of the scribe.'
segments.s578-g29: که introduces the subordinate clause ('that/so that'). چون is temporal 'when'. کودک او 'his child' (possessive). به مردی رسد is a common classical Persian idiom: به + nominal form + رسیدن = 'to reach X' → here 'to reach manhood / to become a man'; رسد is 3rd singular present/subjunctive of رسیدن used in the sense 'attain / become'.
segments.s578-g30: که again links the relative content 'who/that'. دیهیم here denotes rural/local authority or a rustic lord; it functions as a title/role paired with تاج کیی 'the Kayanian (Kiani) crown' (تاج = crown, کیی = Kayanian). را is the definite object marker attached to the coordinated objects, and سزد (impersonal) means 'it befits / it is deserved' — 'who deserves/warrants the rural lordship and the Kayanian crown.'
segments.s578-g31: سپارم as before is 'I entrust' (1st person). همه پادشاهی 'the whole kingship/kingdom' is the object. ورا (variant of 'به او' / 'به را') means 'to him' (indirect object). The clause reads 'I entrust to him the entire kingdom.'
segments.s578-g32: نسازم is ن + سازم, a negative present 'I will not make / I will not appoint'. جز means 'except', از introduces the motive/source 'out of' and نیک‌خواهی here means 'goodwill / benevolent consideration' toward someone; ورا again 'to him'. The sense: 'I will not appoint/make (him king) except out of goodwill toward him.'
segments.s578-g105: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; subject برادر 'the brother' comes first (common in epic/poetic word order). بشنید is a past-tense verb 'he heard'; چندی is an adverbial phrase 'for a while'; گریست is a past-tense verb 'he wept'. The sequence is straightforward: 'When the brother heard, he wept for a while.'
segments.s578-g106: چو again = 'when'; اندرز is the object 'counsel/advice'. بنوشت here functions as a past action 'he wrote' (an archaic/poetic past form of نوشتن), followed by سالی بزیست 'he lived for a year' (سالی indicating duration). The clause links temporally to the previous: 'when he wrote the counsel, he lived a year.'
segments.s578-g107: برفت (here) is a past verb meaning 'he departed/left' (an archaic form; the provided lemma refers to 'snow' but context makes clear it's رفتن). و coordinates the clauses 'and'; بماند is a present/past-subjunctive-ish form meaning 'remained' or 'was left'; این سخن یادگار = 'this saying as a memorial' (این modifies سخن, یادگار is predicate/complement).
segments.s578-g108: تو is the vocative/second-person 'you'; اندر جهان = 'in the world' (اندر = in/within, جهان = this life/world). تخم is a noun 'seed' used metaphorically; زفتی مکار are epithets following it, 'vile/filthy (زفتی)' and 'deceitful (مکار)'. The sequence is an address: 'you, in the world, a vile deceitful seed.'
segments.s578-g109: که introduces the subordinate/explanatory clause 'for/that'; هم here is emphatic 'even'; یک زمان = 'one time/once'; روز تو بگذرد literally 'your day passes' — بگذرد is a present/subjunctive form used here to express an eventuality ('will pass'/'will come to an end').
segments.s578-g110: چنین 'thus/so' links to the consequence. برده functions as a past participle 'having borne' (from بردن) — a verbal adjective describing رنج تو 'the toil/suffering you endured' (note: the provided sense list does not include this verbal sense). دشمن خورد is a compact clause 'the enemy ate/consumed' (خورد literally 'ate' used figuratively: the enemy profited from or consumed that toil).
segments.s578-g111: چو = 'when/as'; آدینه here = 'Friday' (a poetic simile: likening the situation to a Friday); هر مزد = 'every reward/wage'; بهمن is a proper name/epithet meaning auspicious/good (here 'of Bahman' or 'blessed by Bahman'); بود is the past copula 'was'. The clause describes a time/condition: 'when, like a Friday, every reward was auspicious.'
segments.s578-g112: برین is a prepositional compound 'بر + این' = 'for/on this' (here: 'for this matter'); کار 'affair/work' is the noun; فرخ 'fortunate/auspicious' modifies نشیمن 'seat/place/position'; بود is the past copula 'was'. The phrase means 'for this affair there was an auspicious seat/position.'
segments.s578-g49: This is a verbal-nominal phrase built around the infinitive-like form بگذاشتن (here with the poetic prefix ب-). گناه is the thing being removed ("sin"), and از marks the source or separation ('from/of'), so the whole phrase means to remove or let go of the sin that pertains to the گنهکار. گنهکار is the person affected ("the sinner").
segments.s578-g50: پی + نگه‌داشتن here expresses attending to or guarding a group; پی functions as a directional/adverbial element ('following, after') and مردمی را is the definite object ('the people') marked by را. نگه‌داشتن is used in the sense 'to preserve/guard' or 'to keep watch over.'
segments.s578-g51: هرانکس که introduces a relative clause 'whoever.' The pronoun او is a classical/emphatic subject pronoun repeating the subject (common in older Persian). این هنرها is the object of بجست, an archaic past-tense form meaning 'sought.' Overall: 'whoever sought these arts/virtues.'
segments.s578-g52: This clause states a requirement using the impersonal/modal verb باید ('must' / 'it is necessary'). خرد is the thing required ('wisdom'), followed by a coordinated list حزم و رای درست ('prudence and correct/proper counsel'). درِست modifies رای. Word order is noun + modal (typical in poetry) with the sense 'one ought to have X, Y, and Z.'
segments.s578-g53: بباید is the modal 'must/ought' (classical form). خرد شاه را is the direct object phrase 'the king's wisdom' (شاه in apposition to خرد; را marks the definite object), and ناگزیر is a predicate/adjectival complement meaning 'inevitable/indispensable.' The construction states necessity for the king's wisdom.
segments.s578-g54: هم introduces an additional requirement ('also'). آموزش is the noun 'instruction/teaching,' and مرد برنا و پیر is a coordinated noun phrase 'the young (vigorous) man and the old (man),' where مرد is the head and برنا و پیر are descriptive adjectives. The verb is understood from the previous clause (elliptical): '(instruction) is also necessary for both young and old.'
segments.s578-g55: دل پادشا is a genitive/possessive phrase 'the king's heart' (classical juxtaposition instead of ezāfe). چون is a subordinating conjunction 'when/if/as,' and گراید is an older present/optative form meaning 'turns/ inclines.' به introduces the object of inclination (مهر 'affection, kindness').
segments.s578-g56: This clause states the consequence. برو is a poetic/archaic connective here (etymologically from the verb 'go') functioning like 'then' or as a linker between clauses. کامها ('desires/pleasures') is plural and تازه ('fresh/new') qualifies them; دارد is the present-tense 'has/possesses,' and سپهر ('the sky/heavens') is the syntactic subject. Literally: 'then the heavens have fresh desires.'
segments.s578-g17: چنین modifies گفت and means 'thus/so'; گفت is the main verb 'said' (here placed before the subject in a slightly elevated/poetic word order). پس is a temporal/discourse particle 'then'. شاه is the subject 'the king'. با + name (با اردشیر) marks the addressee ('to Ardashir') — in older/poetic Persian با can introduce the person spoken to.
segments.s578-g18: که‌ای is an archaic/vocative form (که + ای) used to address someone: 'O ...'. گرد and چابک‌سوار are coordinated epithets joined by و ('and'); they both modify the addressee. چابک‌سوار is a compound noun 'nimble-rider / swift horseman'. دلیر is a final adjective 'brave' applied to the person addressed. Overall this is a vocative phrase addressing Ardashir with a string of epithets.
segments.s578-g19: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). با من is the partner of the covenant ('with me'). از داد is a genitival/qualifying phrase 'of/concerned with justice' (داد = justice), modifying پیمان 'covenant'. پیمان is the object of کنی; کنی is the 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive (in a conditional clause, 'you do/make'), so the clause means 'if you make a just covenant with me'.
segments.s578-g20: زبان را is a definite direct object ('the tongue' = speech; را marks it as definite). به پیمان گروگان is a prepositional/instrumental phrase 'as a hostage to the pact' (گروگان = hostage, پیمان = pact). The verb کنی repeats and is parallel to the verb in the previous clause (2nd-person subjunctive), completing the conditional: 'and (if) you place your tongue as a hostage to the covenant.'
segments.s578-g21: که begins a subordinate clause ('that' / 'so that'); فرزند من is the subject 'my son'. چون here introduces a temporal clause 'when' (poetic/archaic use). به + مردی + رسد is a verbal phrase meaning 'reaches manhood' — مردی is an abstract noun 'manliness/manhood' and رسد is the 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive/optative 'he reaches/attains'.
segments.s578-g22: به گاه = 'at the time/season of' (به + گاه), so this gives the temporal setting of رسد. دلیری is an abstract noun 'bravery/valor' and گردی here is a coordinated noun with دلیری (joined by و) denoting another quality/time (in epic context often related to riding/warfare or roaming). رسد repeats as the verb 'attains/arrives at'. The whole phrase specifies the time when the son will be ready: 'at the time of valor and of ...'.
segments.s578-g23: سپاری is the verb 'I will entrust / (I) entrust' (in narrative it functions as a future/gnomic action). بدو is the dative 'to him'. تخت و گنج و سپاه are a coordinated list of direct objects 'the throne, the treasure, and the army' that are being entrusted. Word order places the verb first for emphasis, common in epic style.
segments.s578-g24: تو is the addressee 'you'. دستور باشی is an idiomatic predicate meaning 'you shall be the commander/in charge' — دستور functions as 'command/authority' and باشی is the 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive/imperative form of 'to be' ('be/you shall be'). ورا is an older object/dative pronoun meaning 'to him' (equivalent to به او). نیک‌خواه is a substantive/adjective meaning 'well-wisher' or 'benevolent (toward him)'. The phrase orders the addressee to act as commander and friend to the son.
segments.s578-g33: چو + past verb (بشنید) forms a temporal clause 'when he heard'. The verb precedes the proper name (بشنید شاپور), a common poetic word order; پیش مهان is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of the guests' (پیش = 'before/in front of', مهان = 'guests/visitors').
segments.s578-g34: بدو is the dative pronoun 'to him' (به + او) placed before the verb داد ('gave'). داد is a past-tense verb 'gave'. دیهیم is a noun directly following the verb as one object; و links the two objects, and مهر شهان is a genitive-like phrase 'the favour/kindness (مهر) of the king(s)' where شهان qualifies مهر.
segments.s578-g35: چنین گفت is a formula 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'said'); پس 'then/afterwards' places the action in sequence. شاه با اردشیر: با + name marks the addressee ('to/with Ardashir') — in this construction با functions like 'to' (addressed to Ardashir).
segments.s578-g36: که introduces the content clause 'that/which'. کار جهان is a noun phrase 'the affairs/work of the world' (کار = affair/work, جهان = world). بر دل آسان مگیر is an idiomatic negative imperative: مگیر (from گرفتن) = 'do not take'; بر دل = 'upon the heart' (= 'to heart'); آسان = 'easy/light' — together: 'do not take the world's affairs lightly to heart.'
segments.s578-g37: بدان‌ای is an imperative 'know this' with the vocative clitic -ای addressing برادر ('O brother'). که begins the explanatory subordinate clause. بیداد شاه is a noun phrase 'the king's injustice/oppression' (بیداد = injustice, شاه = king) serving as the subject of the following clause.
segments.s578-g38: پی + noun (پی پادشاهی) expresses the idea 'in pursuit of / after kingship' (پی = 'in pursuit of' here). ندارد is third-person singular present 'does not have' or 'has not'; نگاه functions as a noun meaning 'regard/consideration' or 'attention' — together: 'has no regard for pursuing kingship.'
segments.s578-g39: به introduces the circumstance 'in/for'. آگندن here is used as a verbal/nominal form meaning 'to fill/imbue/accumulate' (poetic/archaic usage) and takes گنج ('treasure') as its object; شادان بود is a predicative phrase 'was joyful' (شادان = joyful, بود = was).
segments.s578-g40: به introduces the second clause. زفتی is an adjective/noun 'filth/nastiness' qualifying the following سر (head); سر سرفرازان = 'the heads of the proud/victorious' (سرفرازان = proud/victorious) and بود is the copula 'was' — 'there was filth on the heads of the proud' (poetic inversion of normal word order).
segments.s578-g81: نکوهیده باشد جفا پیشه مرد is a predicative clause: نکوهیده باشد is a subjunctive/jussive-style statement 'is (to be) censured/blameworthy' (باشَد used in an archaic/poetic optative sense). جفا پیشه is a compound in which پیشه (literally 'one who practices') modifies جفا ('injustice/cruelty') to mean 'one who practises injustice', and مرد is the head noun 'man'. Word order places the predicate (نکوهیده باشد) before the noun phrase for emphasis, typical of older Persian poetry.
segments.s578-g82: به گرد در آزداران مگرد is an admonition. مگرد is an archaic negative imperative 'do not turn/go' (here 'do not go about'); به گرد = 'around/about' and در here denotes the palace gate/court. آزداران is plural 'those who hurt/oppose' (the oppressive ones). The prepositional phrase modifies the implied second-person subject of the prohibition.
segments.s578-g83: بدان‌ای برادر که از شهریار opens with an imperative-address: بدان‌ای is 'know this' (imperative + vocative particle), برادر is the form of address 'O brother'. که introduces the content clause 'that ...', and از شهریار marks source/agent 'from the king' (from/of the ruler).
segments.s578-g84: بجوید خردمند هرگونه کار is the content clause: بجوید is a present tense verb 'seeks/searches for' (third-singular), خردمند is the subject/adjective 'the wise (man)' placed after the verb (poetic inversion), and هرگونه کار 'every kind of work/service' is the object. The verb governs the entire noun phrase.
segments.s578-g85: یکی آنک پیروزگر باشد اوی is a relative-style clause meaning 'One who is/if he is victorious'. یکی 'one' introduces a type or example; آنک = 'who/that'; پیروزگر 'victor/triumphant' is a nominative predicate; باشد is subjunctive/colloquial 'is' (used poetically); اوی is the pronoun 'he'. The clause has a somewhat resumptive pronoun (اوی) for emphasis.
segments.s578-g86: ز دشمن نتابد گه جنگ روی: ز دشمن 'against/from the enemy'; نتابد is negative present of تابیدن here with the sense 'to yield/submit/stand down' (so 'does not yield to the enemy'); گه 'sometimes' introduces the following adverbial; جنگ روی literally 'turns his face to battle' (روی 'face' governs a verbal sense 'turn toward'). The clause says the victorious man neither submits to foes nor avoids going into combat at times.
segments.s578-g87: دگر آنک لشکر بدارد به داد begins another example: دگر 'another' + آنک 'who/that' introduces the clause. لشکر بدارد is 'keeps/has an army' (بدارد = 'has/keeps' in present), and به داد 'for justice' ('to enforce/for the sake of justice'), so the phrase describes one who maintains troops to uphold justice.
segments.s578-g88: بداند فزونی مرد نژاد is a short declarative: بداند here means 'he deems/considers' (evaluative sense), فزونی 'preeminence/superiority' modifies the noun phrase مرد نژاد 'men of lineage/noble birth'. Word order places the verb before its object for rhetorical weight: 'he considers the superiority of men of noble stock.'
segments.s578-g1: This clause uses ز + name to mark cause: ز شاپور = 'because of Shapur'. زان‌گونه شد روزگار is a verbal predicate with زان‌گونه (a deictic/adverb 'thus/so') modifying شد ('became') and روزگار as the subject/predicate nominal ('the times' / 'the course of things'). Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect (cause fronted, then result).
segments.s578-g2: که introduces a result/relative clause ('such that/that'), then a locative phrase در باغ با گل ('in a garden with/among flowers') followed by the negative past verb ندیدند ('they did not see') and the object خار ('thorn'). The verb appears before the final object for emphasis; ندیدند is perfective past negative.
segments.s578-g3: A trisected causal list introduced by repeated ز ('from/because of') — ز داد و ز رای و ز آهنگ اوی — where each noun is possessed by the archaic possessive اوی ('his'). داد here means 'justice' (not the verb 'gave'); رای and آهنگ mean 'counsel/plan' and 'intention/aim' respectively. The repeated ز is a poetic coordination marker.
segments.s578-g4: Another causal series beginning with ز: ز بس کوشش و جنگ و نیرنگ اوی — 'from/through so much effort and war and trickery of his.' بس here functions adverbially ('so much', 'many') modifying کوشش. نیرنگ is best read as 'stratagem/deception'; اوی again marks possession.
segments.s578-g5: مر او را is an older/dialectal dative-like construction meaning 'for him' or 'to him' (مر = 'to/for'); او را has a topical/emphatic را. به هر بوم denotes location ('in every land' / 'in every dwelling'), and نماند is the negative perfective 'did not remain' — together: 'for him no enemy remained in any land.'
segments.s578-g6: بدی را is topicalized ('evil' as the theme, marked by را). به گیتی نشیمن is a locative-possessive phrase 'a dwelling/seat in the world' and نماند ('did not remain') negates existence: 'evil had no dwelling-place left in the world.' Note the verbless existential sense achieved by نماند.
segments.s578-g7: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. نومید شد او = 'he became hopeless/despairing' (نومید = 'despairing/hopeless', شد = 'became'). ز چرخ بلند is an ablative/genitive-idiom 'of/from the high heaven' meaning 'despairing of heaven' — a poetic expression.
segments.s578-g8: بشد here conveys 'it became/it came to pass' with سالیانش ('his years' — سالیان + possessive -ش). به هفتاد و اند = 'to seventy and more'; به introduces the quantity reached, and اند is an archaic poetic particle indicating 'and (some)/more'. The whole means 'his years amounted to seventy and a bit.'
segments.s578-g73: هران (هر آن) is an archaic/poetic compound meaning 'every' or 'any' (lit. 'that every'). پادشا is the noun 'king'. کو here functions as an old relative particle 'who/which' (common in classical Persian). جزین = 'جز + این' means 'except this'; راه is 'road/way'; جست is the past stem of جستن ('to seek/take'), so the clause literally reads 'any king who sought a way other than this.'
segments.s578-g74: ز here means 'because of' / 'for the sake of' (poetic usage). نیکیش = نیک + the third-person possessive -ش 'his goodness/virtue'. باید is the impersonal/modal 'must/ought to'. دل و دست is a coordinated object 'heart and hand' (heart = inner intention, hand = deeds/power). شست is the past/infinitival form of شستن ('to wash') used after باید to express obligation: 'must wash.'
segments.s578-g75: ز کشورش = 'from his country/realm' (ز = 'from', کشور + -ش possessive). بپراگند is a jussive/imperative-form verb 'scatter / drive away' (the prefix ب- appears in classical Persian forms to form the shape); زیردست here means 'subordinates / underlings' and is the object of the verb.
segments.s578-g76: همان = 'that one' / 'the same' (pointing back). از درش literally 'from his door' or 'from his court' (در here = door/gate = metonymy for court); ش is his. مرد is 'man/person' and خسروپرست is a compound meaning 'king-worshipper' i.e. a sycophant/flatterer of kings; the phrase identifies such a courtier.
segments.s578-g77: نبینی is the negative present subjunctive/imperative-styled rhetorical question 'do you not see' (نـ- + بینی). که introduces the content clause 'what the wise says'. دانا = 'the wise one'. چه here is the interrogative/complement 'what'. گوید is the third-person present of گفتن 'he says'; همی is a classical particle meaning 'always/still' that gives a continuous/characteristic sense.
segments.s578-g78: دلت را places the object first: دل + second-person suffix -ت 'your heart' + را (definite object marker). ز here = 'from' (removal 'from crookedness'). کژی = 'crookedness/wrongness' (moral perversion). بشوید is a present/subjunctive/passive-like form meaning 'is washed / will be cleansed' (بـ- prefix + شود/شوید shapes classical verbal inflection); همی again adds continuous/characteristic force ('will/does continually cleanse').
segments.s578-g79: که functions as a relative/complementizer 'that/for whom'. هر = 'every'. شاه = 'king'. کو again is the classical relative 'who/which' (archaic usage). را is the object/topic marker attached here to ستایش; ستایش بود = 'there was praise' or 'was praised' (بود is the past copula). The clause thus says 'who had praise' or 'who was praised.'
segments.s578-g80: همه کارش = 'all his work/affairs' (همه 'all', کار 'affairs', -ش 'his'). اندر = classical preposition 'in/within'. فزایش = 'increase/augmentation' (here idiomatically 'prosperity, success, growth'). بود is the past copula 'was', so the clause means 'all his affairs were in increase/ prospered.'
segments.s578-g89: کسی 'کز در پادشاهی بود' is a relative clause: 'کسی' (someone) + 'کز' (archaic/compressed relative 'who/that') introduces the clause. Word order is subject + relative clause with the copula 'بود' (here general/past copular use) at the end: 'who was/is in kingship'.
segments.s578-g90: This clause 'نخواهد که مهتر سپاهی بود' means 'will not want to be the army-chief.' 'نخواهد' is the negative of خواستن ('to want/wish'); 'که' introduces the content clause; 'مهتر سپاهی' = 'chief/leader of the army' (noun + military adjective); 'بود' is the copula meaning 'to be' (here part of the subordinate infinitival/content clause: 'to be').
segments.s578-g91: 'چهارم' numbers this point ('fourth'). 'که' introduces the clause. 'با زیردستان خویش' = 'with his subordinates'; here 'خوی' + the enclitic 'ش' together form the possessive 'خویش' ('his own'), split for metrical reasons.
segments.s578-g92: 'همان باگهر در پرستان خویش' is a simile: 'همان' ('just/like that') + 'باگهر' (poetic adjective 'jeweled/precious') = 'like a jewel'; 'در پرستان خویش' = 'among his followers/retainers' (lit. in/among his worshippers/adherents), again with the split possessive 'خوی'+'ش'.
segments.s578-g93: 'ندارد در گنج را بسته سخت' literally orders as Verb + prepositional object: 'ندارد' (he does not have/keep) + 'در گنج را' (the treasure/treasure-chest as definite object marked by 'را') + 'بسته سخت' (poetically inverted adjective phrase 'tightly closed'). In this construction 'ندارد' negates possession/maintenance of a tightly closed treasure.
segments.s578-g94: 'همی بارد از شاخ بار درخت' describes a continuous action: 'همی' marks ongoing/continual action; 'بارد' here is a poetic/archaic verb meaning 'falls/drops (keeps falling)' (not the modern adjective 'cold'); 'از شاخ بار درخت' = 'from the tree's laden bough' (از = from, شاخ = bough/branch, بار = load/fruit of the bough, درخت = tree).
segments.s578-g95: 'بباید در پادشاهی سپاه' uses the impersonal/modal 'بباید' meaning 'one must/ought to'; word order places the necessity before the domain: 'in kingship/kingdom (در پادشاهی) an army (سپاه) [is required]'.
segments.s578-g96: 'سپاهی در گنج دارد نگاه' continues the practical point: 'سپاهی' (soldiers/troops) + 'در گنج' (in the treasury) + 'دارد' (he has/keeps) + 'نگاه' (here used in the sense 'keeping/guard/maintenance'—an older/poetic nominal use). Together: keep soldiers (or their pay) in the treasury. Note 'نگاه' as 'maintenance' is a poetic/older meaning not fully reflected in the simple dictionary senses given.
segments.s578-g9: بفرمود تا introduces a commanded subordinate clause ('he ordered that...'); بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb 'he said/ordered'. پیش او is a prepositional phrase 'in his presence/before him' and شد (literal 'became/came to be') functions here as the main past verb meaning 'was brought/came' with دبیر (predicate noun) following it — typical Persian order where the predicate noun appears after the verb.
segments.s578-g10: This is an appositive/name string identifying the scribe: 'ابا موبد موبدان اردشیر' — a sequence of titles/epithets and the proper name. There is no verb here; the whole phrase names the person brought forward.
segments.s578-g11: جوانی 'a young man' is followed by a relative clause introduced by که ('who/that'). کهتر برادرش بود = 'who was his younger brother' with کهتر ('smaller/younger') modifying برادرش and بود as the past copula 'was'.
segments.s578-g12: به داد و خرد is a prepositional phrase 'with/to justice and wisdom' (i.e. 'in justice and wisdom'); بر سر افسرش بود is the verbal clause 'was upon his helmet' (بر 'on/upon' + سر 'top/head' + افسرش 'his helmet' + بود 'was'). The whole phrase is figurative: justice and wisdom 'sat on' his helmet (i.e. adorned/characterized him).
segments.s578-g13: ورا نام بود is a common classical copular construction meaning 'his name was...': ورا is a dative 'to him' and نام بود is 'was the name'. The proper name اردشیر follows, with جوان as an epithet 'the young'.
segments.s578-g14: توانا و دانا are coordinate epithets 'capable and wise'. به سود و زیان is a prepositional phrase 'in profit and loss' (= 'in matters of gain and loss'), modifying those qualities to mean he was competent both in good and ill (practical and legal matters).
segments.s578-g15: This is a compact nominal phrase: پسر بد 'an ill/bad son', یکی 'a certain' introduces an indefinite person, خرد here means 'small/young' (i.e. a child), and شاپور نام gives the personal name in apposition ('named Shapur'). Word order places attributes and the indefinite marker before the proper name.
segments.s578-g16: هنوز 'still/yet' + از جهان نارسیده is a clause meaning 'not yet come into the world' (نارسیده = 'premature/not arrived'). به کام 'to (his) desire' idiomatically indicates fulfillment; together the phrase says he had not yet come into the world to satisfy/fulfill his desires.
segments.s578-g113: می‌لعل (object) = 'ruby-red wine' placed before the verb for emphasis; پیش + آور is a verb phrase meaning 'bring before / bring forward' with پیش as a directional/adverbial element and آور as the imperative. ای هاشمی is a vocative: the poet addresses someone as 'O Hashemi' (written here joined as آور‌ای هاشمی), so the whole segment reads as an imperative request 'Bring the mi-lal forward, O Hashemi.'
segments.s578-g114: ز introduces a cause or source ('from' / 'because of'); خمی is the noun 'wrath/anger' being described. که introduces a relative/subordinate clause qualifying that wrath. هرگز نگیرد is negative present meaning 'never takes/accepts' (i.e. 'never abates'); کمی = 'a little' (what it does not accept). Together: 'from a wrath that never yields even a little.'
segments.s578-g115: چو = 'when'; the numeral phrase شست و سه (poetic/archaic ordering) = 'sixty‑three' (here indicating age). سال شد is the idiom 'years became' = 'he turned (years old)'; گوش کر is a compound predicate 'ear‑deaf' (i.e. 'became deaf'). Poetic inversion and repeated شد reflect classical Persian word order: 'When he became sixty‑three years, [then] he became deaf of hearing.'
segments.s578-g116: ز بیشی = 'because of / from surplus/excess' (a prepositional phrase giving a reason or context). چرا is the interrogative 'why'; جویم is first‑person present/subjunctive 'should I seek / I seek' used here rhetorically. آیین و فر are the objects 'ritual/ceremony and (royal) glory'. The clause asks rhetorically why the speaker should pursue ceremony and glory given surplus/what is already had.
segments.s578-g117: کنون = 'now' (temporal adverb). داستانهای is the plural noun 'tales/legends'; شاه اردشیر is a genitive/title phrase 'King Ardashir' (epic style often juxtaposes title and name without an explicit ezafe). The whole noun phrase = 'now, the tales of King Ardashir.'
segments.s578-g118: بگویم here expresses the speaker's intention 'I will tell / let me tell' (first person volitive/subjunctive). ز گفتار من = 'from my speech / from my words' (prepositional phrase indicating source). یادگیر is an imperative 'learn / remember' addressed to the listener, so the full clause means 'I will tell — learn (them) from what I say.'
segments.s578-g41: 'خنک شاه' places the adjective before the noun (typical in Persian poetry) = 'a calm/pleasant king.' 'باداد' is an archaic optative/predicate meaning 'may he be' or 'be granted' (used here as a wish), and the conjunction 'و' links a second predicate 'یزدان‌پرست' (= 'pious/God‑worshipper'), so the whole segment reads as a single wish about the king.
segments.s578-g42: 'کزو' (from whom) ties this clause back to the king; 'شاد باشد' uses the subjunctive/optative 'باشد' (here 'may be/have'), so 'may be happy.' 'دل زیردست' is a possessive/genitive grouping meaning 'the hearts of the underlings/subjects' (دل = heart + زیردست = subordinates). Together: 'from whom the hearts of his subjects may be glad.'
segments.s578-g43: The repeated 'به' introduces the two spheres in which he increases: 'به داد و به بخشش' = 'in justice and in giving.' 'فزونی کند' is a verb phrase with 'فزونی' (increase) as object/nominal and 'کند' as present tense 'he makes/causes,' so 'he increases (things) in justice and generosity.'
segments.s578-g44: 'جهان را' is the definite object marked by 'را' = 'the world.' 'بدین رهنمونی' is the fused prepositional phrase 'بدین' = 'by this' + 'رهنمونی' = 'guidance/instruction,' and 'کند' is the verb 'he makes/does' (here 'he directs'). So the clause means 'he directs/the world by this guidance.' (Note: in the provided word list 'دین' was listed separately; here it functions as part of the fused 'بدین' meaning 'by this'.)
segments.s578-g45: 'نگه دارد' is the verb phrase 'keeps/guards' (نگه = keeping/watch + دارد = holds/keeps). The prepositional 'از دشمنان کشورش' = 'from the enemies of his country' is the object/complement meaning 'he protects (it) from the country's enemies.' The possessive suffix 'ش' on 'کشور' marks 'his country.'
segments.s578-g46: The preposition 'به' with 'ابر اندر آرد' gives a motion sense 'to/into the cloud(s) he brings/raises' where 'اندر آرد' = 'brings/puts into.' 'سر و افسرش' are coordinated objects ('head and his afsar'); 'افسرش' carries the possessive suffix 'ش.' Together they form a vivid image: he raises his head and helmet up among the clouds (i.e. holds them high).
segments.s578-g47: The parallel prepositional phrases 'به داد و به آرام' = 'with justice and with calm' modify the following nominal 'گنج آگند.' 'آگند' is an adjectival/predicative form meaning 'filled' or 'replete,' so the line says the treasury (گنج, here 'treasury/treasure‑house') became filled by these qualities; note the verb 'to be' is often omitted in classical Persian and is supplied by context.
segments.s578-g48: 'به بخشش' = 'by (his) giving/generosity.' 'ز دل رنج' = 'the pain/sorrow from the heart(s)' (z = from). 'بپراگند' is an old present/prefixed form meaning 'he scatters/disperses' (here third‑person present), so the clause means 'by giving he disperses sorrow from the heart.'
segments.s578-g97: If-clause with present-second-person verb: 'اگر' introduces a condition; 'گنجت' is گنج + enclitic -ت = 'your treasure'; 'آباد' is an adjective 'prosperous/kept in order'; 'داری' is the present-tense 2nd‑person singular of داشتن ('you have/keep'). The short prepositional phrase 'به داد' follows and means 'with/by justice' (behaving justly) modifying the whole clause.
segments.s578-g98: Main-result clause in which the pronoun 'تو' (you) is stated first; the preposition 'از' marks cause ('because of' here) so 'تو از گنج شاد' = 'you are happy because of the treasure.' The conjunction 'و' links a second clause 'سپاه از تو شاد' where again 'از' indicates cause ('the army is glad on account of you'). Word order places the causal phrase before the adjective 'شاد'.
segments.s578-g99: 'سلیحت' is سلاح with the enclitic -ت = 'your weapon(s)'; 'در آرایش' is a locative phrase 'in/within the array/arrangement' (آرایش here = 'array, arraying' i.e. order or battle‑array); 'خویش' (here written خوی + ش) is the reflexive/possessive element 'one's own' (خوی + enclitic ش = 'its/one's own'). Finally 'دار' is an imperative/hortative 'keep/hold' (poetic/archaic use related to داشتن) — the verb appears after the locative phrase, producing the sense 'keep your weapon in its proper array.'
segments.s578-g100: 'سزد' is an impersonal present meaning 'it befits' or 'it is fitting'; the following noun phrase 'کت شب تیره' ('a coat for the dark night') gives what is fitting. 'آید' ('comes') plus the light locative 'به کار' ('into use' / 'to be of use') is a fixed collocation meaning 'comes into play / will be needed.' Overall: 'it is fitting to have a coat when the dark night comes into use.'
segments.s578-g101: 'بس' here is an admonitory 'enough / do not (be over‑...)' functioning to warn; 'ایمن مشو' is a negative imperative 'do not become secure/safe' (مشو = 'do not become'). The prepositional phrase 'بر نگهدار خویش' means 'in/against your keeper/guardian' (نگهدار = keeper/guardian) with خویش as possessive; the line cautions against excessive reliance on one's guardian.
segments.s578-g102: 'چو' = 'when'; 'ایمن شدی' = 'you have become safe/secure' (شدی = 2nd‑person perfect/become). 'راست کن' is an imperative 'make right / set straight' (راست = 'right/straight', کن = 'do'); 'کار خویش' = 'your affairs' (کار = affairs, خویش = reflexive/possessive). Thus: when you are safe, put your affairs in order.
segments.s578-g103: 'سرانجام' is an adverb/noun meaning 'in the end/finally'; 'مرگ' is the subject 'death'; the verb 'آیدت' is 'comes to you' (آید + enclitic -ت). 'بی‌گمان' is an adverb 'undoubtedly.' The clause is a plain declarative: 'In the end, death will come to you, undoubtedly.'
segments.s578-g104: 'اگر' = 'if'; 'تیره‌ای‌گر' is a compound/agentive meaning roughly 'one who darkens' or 'a sower/bring­er of gloom' (poetic/archaic formation); 'چراغ جهان' = 'the lamp of the world' (a poetic epithet). The short clause poses a hypothetical: 'even if you are a darkener of the world's lamp.'
segments.s578-g65: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. روشن نباشد is a negative present/subjunctive clause meaning 'is not clear' (classical/poetic negative: نباشد = 'is not' / 'be not'), and بپراگند is a b‑prefixed jussive/present form of پراگندَن used here in a narrative/permissive sense ('it scatters' / 'let it scatter'). The implied subject of بپراگند is the spirit/inner element picked up in the next segment.
segments.s578-g66: تن بی‌روان را is the definite object: تن 'body' modified by the adjective phrase بی‌روان 'devoid of soul/ lifeless', with را marking it as the direct object. به خاک is a directional prepositional phrase ('onto/to the earth') and افگند is the past 3rd‑person verb 'threw/cast'. The clause states the action done to the (now) soulless body.
segments.s578-g67: چنین همچو is a pleonastic pair meaning 'thus/like that'. شد is the past verb 'became' or 'came to be', and شاه بیدادگر is the predicate noun phrase 'a tyrant/cruel king'. Word order is subject (implicit or contextual) + predicate + copula verb in classical narrative.
segments.s578-g68: جهان زو شود literally 'the world from him becomes' — ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of' and places causation on 'him'. شود is a present/subjunctive form used in narrative to mean 'was made / became'. زود is an adverb 'soon/quickly', and the idiomatic pair زیر و زبر means 'under and over' i.e. 'upside down' or 'overturned'. The whole line: the world was quickly overturned because of him.
segments.s578-g69: بدوبر means 'twofold' or 'in two ways' (i.e. doubled), and پس از مرگ is the temporal phrase 'after death' (پس از = 'after' + مرگ 'death'). نفرین بود is a verbless/existential clause with بود (past of 'to be') meaning 'there was a curse'. The sequence states that after death a double curse existed.
segments.s578-g70: همان نام 'that very name' or 'the same reputation' introduces the label; او is the pronoun 'he', and شاه بی‌دین is a nominal phrase 'king without religion / impious king'. بود is the past copula 'was'. The line assigns the disparaging name to him.
segments.s578-g71: بدین (به + این 'to/for this') ... و بدان (به + آن 'to/for that') is a paired locative/contrastive construction 'in this ... and in that ...'. دار here functions as 'place/holder' (i.e. 'let this be the eye / this shall serve as the eye') and چشم / گوش are the nouns 'eye' and 'ear'. The line uses a verbless/nominal equative poetic construction: 'make this the eye and that the ear.'
segments.s578-g72: که is the relative conjunction 'who/that'. اویست is an archaic/contracted copular form (classical spelling of 'او است' = 'he is') and introduces the identification; دارنده is the present‑participle 'possessor/holder', and جان و هوش are the nouns 'life (soul)' and 'intellect/awareness'. The clause says 'who is the possessor of life and sense.'

sentences.s578-s29: Let the body under command be sinful, unless the people are pure and God‑worshipping.
sentences.s578-s30: The people's heart and brain are two kings of the body; the other organs are the body's army.
sentences.s578-s31: When the people's brain and heart became defiled, by despair of counsel they became cleansed.
sentences.s578-s32: At that body the soul becomes frantic; the army, like Zayd the king, is without a champion.
sentences.s578-s13: I entrust this royal crown to you; likewise I place the same treasure and army with you.
sentences.s578-s14: Ardashir's words he accepted from him, before the nobles and before the scribe.
sentences.s578-s15: That when his child attains manhood, (when) he becomes a man who is worthy of the rural lordship and of the Kayanian crown.
sentences.s578-s16: I entrust all the kingdom to him; I will not make (him king) except out of goodwill toward him.
sentences.s578-s53: When the brother heard it, he wept for a while; when he wrote the counsel, he lived for a year.
sentences.s578-s54: He departed, and this saying remained as your memorial; you, in the world, a vile deceitful seed.
sentences.s578-s55: For even once your day will pass; thus the toil you have borne the enemy consumed.
sentences.s578-s56: When, as on a Friday, every reward was of Bahman, for this affair there was a fortunate seat.
sentences.s578-s25: To pardon the sinner and to watch over the people.
sentences.s578-s26: Whoever sought these virtues must have wisdom, prudence, and sound counsel.
sentences.s578-s27: A king must inevitably possess wisdom; likewise, instruction is needed for both the young man and the old.
sentences.s578-s28: When the king's heart inclines toward affection, the heavens gain fresh pleasures.
sentences.s578-s9: Thus the king then said to Ardashir, "O swift and nimble rider, brave one!"
sentences.s578-s10: If you make a covenant of justice with me and put your tongue as a hostage to the pact,
sentences.s578-s11: that when my son reaches manhood and comes to a season of valor and of (military) roaming,
sentences.s578-s12: I will entrust to him the throne, the treasure, and the army; you shall be his commander and a well-wisher toward him.
sentences.s578-s17: When Shapur heard (this), he, in the presence of the guests, gave to him the villagers and the kingly favour.
sentences.s578-s18: Thus the king said to Ardashir: 'Do not take the affairs of the world lightly to your heart.'
sentences.s578-s19: Know this, O brother, that a king's injustice pays no heed to (the pursuit of) kingship.
sentences.s578-s20: He rejoiced in filling his treasure; the proud had filthy heads.
sentences.s578-s41: A man given to injustice is blameworthy; do not go about the court among the oppressors.
sentences.s578-s42: Know this, O brother, that the wise man seeks every kind of service from the king.
sentences.s578-s43: One who is victorious — he does not yield to the enemy; sometimes he turns his face to battle.
sentences.s578-s44: Another is he who keeps an army for justice; he esteems men of noble stock as superior.
sentences.s578-s1: Because of Shapur the times became such that, in a garden full of flowers, no thorn was seen.
sentences.s578-s2: Because of his justice and his counsel and his purpose, and because of so much of his exertion, war, and stratagem.
sentences.s578-s3: For him there remained no enemy in any land; evil had no dwelling-place in the world.
sentences.s578-s4: When he despaired of the lofty heavens, his years reached seventy and more.
sentences.s578-s37: Any king who seeks a path other than this must, for the sake of his goodness, wash his heart and hand.
sentences.s578-s38: From his realm cast out the underlings; that one from his court is a flatterer of kings.
sentences.s578-s39: Do you not see what the wise man always says? He will wash your heart of crookedness.
sentences.s578-s40: For every king who was praised, all his affairs were in increase (i.e., prospering).
sentences.s578-s45: Anyone who is in kingship will not wish to be the army's chief.
sentences.s578-s46: Fourth: with his subordinates he should be like a jewel among his followers.
sentences.s578-s47: He does not keep the treasure tightly shut; from the tree's laden bough it keeps falling.
sentences.s578-s48: In a kingdom one must have an army; one should keep soldiers' pay (or troops) in the treasury.
sentences.s578-s5: He commanded that a scribe be brought before him: Aba Mobed, the mobeds' mobed, Ardashir.
sentences.s578-s6: A young man, who was his younger brother; justice and wisdom sat upon his helmet.
sentences.s578-s7: His name was Ardashir the young, capable and wise in matters of gain and loss.
sentences.s578-s8: A certain ill-favored young son named Shapur, who had not yet come into the world to fulfill his desires.
sentences.s578-s57: Bring the ruby-red wine forward, O Hashemi — for it is from a wrath that never eases even a little.
sentences.s578-s58: When he reached sixty‑three years his ear grew deaf; from surplus, why should I seek ritual and glory?
sentences.s578-s59: Now I will tell the tales of King Ardashir; learn them from my words.
sentences.s578-s21: May the king be calm and God‑worshipping, from whom the hearts of his subjects are glad.
sentences.s578-s22: He increases (abundance) in justice and in generosity, and by this guidance he directs the world.
sentences.s578-s23: He keeps watch against the enemies of his country, and he lifts his head and helmet into the clouds.
sentences.s578-s24: With justice and with calm the treasury was filled; by giving he scatters sorrow from the heart.
sentences.s578-s49: If you keep your treasure prosperous and act with justice, you will be glad because of the treasure, and the army will be glad because of you.
sentences.s578-s50: Keep your weapon in its array; it is fitting to have a coat when the dark night comes to hand.
sentences.s578-s51: Do not be too confident in your keeper; when you become secure, set your affairs straight.
sentences.s578-s52: In the end, death will come to you without doubt; even if you are a darkener of the world's lamp.
sentences.s578-s33: When the spirit was no longer clear, he let it scatter and cast the lifeless body to the earth.
sentences.s578-s34: Thus he became a tyrant king; the world because of him was soon turned upside down.
sentences.s578-s35: After his death there was a twofold curse; that name — he was a godless king.
sentences.s578-s36: Make this the eye, and make that the ear; for he is the possessor of life and awareness.
